trace.rationale: Historical data shows that in MLB games, the first inning has a run scored approximately 55% of the time, based on general patterns from pre-cutoff seasons. No specific team or pitcher adjustments are available, so this baseline is used.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 52% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific team or pitcher context provided to adjust this baseline.
Historical data shows roughly 50-55% of MLB games have a run scored in the first inning, with no strong evidence to deviate from that baseline.

Historical MLB data shows roughly 50-55% of first innings have at least one run, with slight variation by team strength. No specific team or pitcher info provided, so baseline estimate.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific evidence here to deviate from that baseline.

Historical data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run in the first inning across MLB games, with slight variation by team strength and pitcher. No specific game context provided, so baseline estimate.

Historical data shows roughly 50-55% of MLB first innings have at least one run scored, with slight variation based on team strengths and starting pitchers. No specific game details are provided, so the base rate is the best estimate.
